subtopics.title: Definition and Functions of Literature
common_misconceptions: Literature is only written texts
Literature is just fiction
Only academic scholars can study literature
subtopics.teaching_note: Literature = creative expression of human experience through language. Functions: educates about life/society, entertains, preserves culture, criticizes social ills, develops language skills. Nigerian oral literature: folktales (Tortoise stories, Ajapa), proverbs, epics (Sango, Ozidi), oral poetry (_Ijala_, _Rara_). Every ethnic group has rich oral traditions.

subtopics.title: Genres of Literature
common_misconceptions: Poetry must rhyme
Drama is only for stage performance
Prose is easier than poetry (all genres require different skills)
subtopics.teaching_note: Three main genres: Prose (novel, short story - written in paragraphs, everyday language), Poetry (concise, uses imagery, rhythm, meter - written in lines and stanzas), Drama (play - meant for performance, dialogue-driven, stage directions). Sub-genres: Tragedy (sad ending, protagonist falls), Comedy (humorous, happy ending), Tragicomedy (mix of both). Epic: long narrative poem about heroic deeds (e.g., Milton's Paradise Lost).

subtopics.title: Figures of Speech
common_misconceptions: Simile and metaphor are the same (simile uses 'like/as')
Personification is only for animals
Hyperbole is lying
subtopics.teaching_note: Simile: comparison using 'like' or 'as' (e.g., 'He runs like a cheetah'). Metaphor: direct comparison without 'like/as' (e.g., 'He is a lion in battle'). Personification: giving human qualities to non-human things (e.g., 'The sun smiled'). Hyperbole: deliberate exaggeration. Irony: saying the opposite of what is meant. Oxymoron: contradictory terms ('deafening silence'). Alliteration: repetition of initial consonant sounds. Assonance: repetition of vowel sounds.

subtopics.title: Literary Devices in Prose
common_misconceptions: Character and characterization are the same
Setting is only the physical location
First person narrator is always reliable
subtopics.teaching_note: Character: person/animal in a story. Characterization: how author creates characters (direct description, speech, actions, thoughts, other's reactions). Types: protagonist (main character), antagonist (opposes protagonist), round (complex), flat (one-dimensional), dynamic (changes), static (unchanging). Plot structure: exposition → rising action → climax → falling action → resolution. Conflict: internal (man vs self) or external (man vs man, man vs nature, man vs society).

subtopics.title: Poetic Devices and Elements
common_misconceptions: All poems must rhyme
Free verse has no structure at all
The poet is always the speaker in a poem
subtopics.teaching_note: Stanza = group of lines (like a paragraph). Common stanzas: couplet (2 lines), tercet (3), quatrain (4), sestet (6), octave (8). Rhyme scheme: pattern of end rhymes (ABAB, AABB, ABBA). Meter: regular pattern of stressed/unstressed syllables (iambic pentameter = 10 syllables, alternating unstressed/stressed). Enjambment: line continues without pause into next line. Caesura: pause within a line. Tone: poet's attitude. Mood: reader's feeling. Diction: word choice. Symbolism: using objects to represent ideas.

subtopics.title: Things Fall Apart by Chinua Achebe
common_misconceptions: Okonkwo is a perfect hero (he has fatal flaws)
The book is only about colonialism
Achebe is criticizing all of Igbo culture
subtopics.teaching_note: Published 1958. Okonkwo: tragic hero with hamartia (fatal flaw) - fear of weakness/laziness like his father Unoka. Themes: clash of cultures (Igbo vs European), masculinity and its dangers, tradition vs change, fate vs free will, language as a tool of colonialism. Structure: three parts - pre-European (Part 1), arrival of Europeans (Part 2), return and tragedy (Part 3). Famous quote: 'The white man is very clever... put a knife on the things that held us together.' Literary significance: African response to colonial literature like Heart of Darkness.

subtopics.title: Shakespearean Drama
common_misconceptions: Shakespeare is too difficult for SS1 students
Old English was used (it's Early Modern English)
Shakespeare's plays were originally meant to be read
subtopics.teaching_note: William Shakespeare (1564-1616), English playwright and poet. Wrote 37 plays, 154 sonnets. Types: Tragedies (Romeo and Juliet, Macbeth, Hamlet, Othello, King Lear), Comedies (A Midsummer Night's Dream, Twelfth Night), Histories (Julius Caesar, Henry IV). Romeo and Juliet: star-crossed lovers, feud between Montagues and Capulets, themes of love, fate, youth vs age, family conflict. Famous soliloquy: 'To be or not to be' (Hamlet). Dramatic devices: soliloquy (character speaks alone), aside (character speaks to audience), dramatic irony.
